---
title: "Cross-Context & Format Sensitivity"
subtitle: "Equivalent meaning, different surface form, different answer"
---

## Overview

Cross-context consistency measures whether LLM outputs remain stable when semantics are held constant but context form changes: language, template, ordering, punctuation, answer format, or modality. Failures here are especially costly because they make system behavior difficult to predict and replicate.

A broad body of work shows that prompt and template details can produce large swings in performance (e.g., [Zhao et al., 2021](https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.09690); [Lu et al., 2022](https://aclanthology.org/2022.acl-long.556/); [Sclar et al., 2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.11324)). Demonstration order, label token choices, and small textual artifacts can alter outcomes independent of underlying problem difficulty. Multilingual settings add another layer: equivalent factual queries can yield different answers and confidence levels across languages ([Wang et al., 2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.10378)).

In short, many models remain sensitive to surface form. Robust deployment therefore benefits from perturbation testing as a default QA practice rather than a niche robustness exercise.

## Key Papers

### Prompt and Demonstration Sensitivity
**[Calibrate Before Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.09690)** (Zhao et al., 2021) · ICML

> Landmark paper on contextual calibration and few-shot prompt sensitivity.

**[Fantastically Ordered Prompts](https://aclanthology.org/2022.acl-long.556/)** (Lu et al., 2022) · ACL

> Demonstration order alone can strongly affect in-context learning outcomes.

**[Rethinking the Role of Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2202.12837)** (Min et al., 2022) · EMNLP

> Shows superficial format cues can matter more than semantic relevance in ICL.

**[Quantifying Sensitivity to Spurious Prompt Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.11324)** (Sclar et al., 2023) · arXiv

> Capitalization, punctuation, and label tokens can materially shift model behavior.

### Cross-Lingual and Multimodal Consistency
**[Cross-Lingual Consistency of Factual Knowledge in Multilingual LMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.10378)** (Wang et al., 2023) · arXiv

> Direct evidence that factual outputs can diverge by language for equivalent questions.

**[Language Models are Multilingual Chain-of-Thought Reasoners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03057)** (Shi et al., 2023) · ICLR

> CoT transfer exists but is uneven across language-resource conditions.

**[MMBench](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.06281)** (Liu et al., 2023) · arXiv

> Multimodal benchmark exposing prompt/format brittleness in VLM evaluation.

## Preprints & Recent Work

**[PromptBench](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.04528)** (Zhu et al., 2023) · NeurIPS Datasets/Benchmarks track

> Standardized stress testing for adversarial and perturbed prompts.

**[Look at the Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.09309)** (Sahay et al., 2024) · arXiv

> Classifier-free metric for prompt sensitivity and output instability.

**[MMMU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.16502)** (Fu et al., 2023) · arXiv

> Broad multimodal reasoning benchmark useful for cross-format consistency studies.

## Blog Posts & Practitioner Resources

- **[Hugging Face evaluation/robustness guides](https://huggingface.co/blog)** (HF, ongoing)
  Practical tutorials on prompt robustness and multilingual evaluation.

- **[OpenAI Cookbook](https://cookbook.openai.com/)** (OpenAI, ongoing)
  Prompt/format testing patterns and reproducibility practices.

- **[CRFM/HELM docs](https://crfm.stanford.edu/helm/latest/)** (Stanford CRFM, ongoing)
  Multi-scenario framework that encourages robust cross-context reporting.

## Benchmarks & Datasets

| Name | Year | Description | Link |
|------|------|-------------|------|
| [PromptBench](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.04528) | 2023 | Adversarial and perturbed prompt robustness | <https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.04528> |
| [CheckList](https://aclanthology.org/2020.acl-main.442/) | 2020 | Invariance and directional expectation tests | <https://aclanthology.org/2020.acl-main.442/> |
| [MGSM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03057) | 2023 | Multilingual grade-school math consistency | <https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03057> |
| [XCOPA](https://aclanthology.org/2020.findings-emnlp.240/) | 2020 | Cross-lingual causal commonsense | <https://aclanthology.org/2020.findings-emnlp.240/> |
| [Belebele](https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.16884) | 2023 | Massively multilingual reading comprehension | <https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.16884> |
| [MMBench](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.06281) | 2023 | Multimodal prompt/format robustness | <https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.06281> |
| [MMMU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.16502) | 2023 | Multi-discipline multimodal reasoning | <https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.16502> |

## GitHub Repos

- **[microsoft/promptbench](https://github.com/microsoft/promptbench)**
  Core benchmark implementation for prompt perturbation testing.

- **[EleutherAI/lm-evaluation-harness](https://github.com/EleutherAI/lm-evaluation-harness)**
  Reproducible multi-task framework for prompt-format comparisons.

- **[openai/evals](https://github.com/openai/evals)**
  Custom format-variant eval authoring.

## Key Takeaways

::: {.callout-tip}
## Key Insights
- Surface-form changes frequently cause large behavior shifts even when semantics are unchanged.
- Cross-lingual consistency is still a major open reliability problem.
- Robust evaluation should always include prompt/template perturbation sweeps and variance reporting.
:::

## See Also

- [Self-Consistency & Contradiction](self-consistency.qmd)
- [Calibration & Uncertainty](calibration.qmd)
- [Behavioral Consistency & Sycophancy](behavioral-consistency.qmd)
- [Benchmark Reliability](benchmark-reliability.qmd)
